Paralytic polio rates are extremely low in vaccinated populations, though very rare cases of vaccine-associated paralytic poliomyelitis (VAPP) can occur with the oral polio vaccine (OPV). These VAPP cases happen in approximately 1 in 2.7 million OPV doses, more often with the first dose. The risk is much lower with inactivated polio vaccine (IPV), which is a "killed" vaccine and does not use live virus. 
Understanding the Rare Cases of Paralytic Polio After Vaccination
Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV):
This vaccine contains a weakened live virus that, in very rare instances, can revert to a paralytic form, leading to VAPP. This is the most common source of paralytic polio cases in highly vaccinated populations. 
Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (VDPV):
When the OPV virus reverts to a form that can spread and cause paralysis, it's called vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV). These outbreaks are linked to low vaccination rates, allowing the virus to spread. 
The Role of Vaccination in Reducing Paralytic Polio
Near Elimination of Wild Polio:
The global introduction of polio vaccines has been highly successful in nearly eliminating wild poliovirus, especially after WPV2 and WPV3 were eradicated. 
High Vaccine Effectiveness:
With high vaccination coverage, the risk of wild or vaccine-derived paralytic polio becomes extremely low. 
Shift to Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV):
Public health strategies are shifting to IPV in many areas because it uses a "killed" virus and cannot cause VAPP. 
Why Paralytic Polio Can Still Occur
VAPP and Low Coverage:
The risk of VAPP exists with the oral vaccine. When immunization rates fall, even the rare VAPP-causing virus can spread and cause outbreaks. 
Provocation Paralysis:
A rare phenomenon, provocation paralysis, can occur after intramuscular injections, such as shots given for other illnesses, shortly after a child receives the oral polio vaccine, according to a study from PubMed. 
In essence, paralysis after vaccination is exceptionally rare and primarily linked to the live-attenuated OPV. However, this risk is significantly outweighed by the benefit of preventing polio through widespread vaccination.

In spring 1954, the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis organized the largest medical field trial to date, enrolling roughly 1.8 million first-, second-, and third-graders across the United States. Led by Thomas Francis Jr. at the University of Michigan, the study tested Jonas Salk’s inactivated poliovirus vaccine using randomized, placebo-controlled methods in many sites. The first shots followed earlier pilot doses in February 1954; the full-scale trial began on April 26, 1954, in McLean, Virginia.

On April 12, 1955, Francis announced the verdict in Ann Arbor: Salk’s vaccine was “safe, effective, and potent,” preventing paralytic disease by about 80–90%. The finding rapidly transformed public health policy, triggering nationwide immunization and sharply lowering U.S. polio cases within years. Accounts differ on specific site procedures, but sources agree on the scale, oversight, and decisive impact.

Many organochloride compounds, such as BHC and DDT, were first synthesized in the 1800s, but their properties as insecticides were not fully discovered and exploited until the late 1930s. BHC (Benzene hexachloride) was first produced by the English scientist Michael Faraday in 1825, but its properties as an insecticide were not identified until 1944. DDT (dichlorodipheny|trichloroethane) was first prepared by Othmar Ziedler, an Austrian chemist, in 1825, but the Swiss chemist Paul Hermann Müller did not discover DDT's insecticidal properties until 1939 - a discovery that led to Müller's award of the Nobel Prize in 1948.

- Eye Exams: To check for eye damage or microfilariae in the eyes.

“1. The polio story as you learned it is wrong. It’s one of the most often misunderstood sequence of events in the last two hundred years. I wanted to explain a few things about the disease to help people understand what actually happened.
2. The first modern account of something resembling polio was in 1789. A physician named Michael Underwood described an illness in children he called “Debility of the Lower Extremities.” He attributed it to teething and foul bowels.
3. One of the next mentions was from Louisiana in 1841. A few children came down with paralysis. The supposed cause: teething. Why would teething be associated with paralysis?
4. Various stories appeared throughout the 1800s of children coming down with paralysis, almost always in their legs. Many people called it “teething paralysis,” but others settled on “infantile paralysis.”
5. This was a new phenomenon: Doctors had never seen it before and didn’t know why it was happening. Research began to reveal that the cause of paralysis were lesions on the grey part of the spinal cord.
6. If you developed a lesion on your spinal cord, they called this a “poliomyelitis.” Polio = grey. Myelitis = inflammation of the spinal cord.
7. A poliomyelitis was a lesion on your spinal cord. You could have more than one of them. But they didn’t know why children had begun developing them, seemingly out of nowhere
8. Scientists conducted research on animals by purposefully poisoning them with arsenic, an ingredient of popular medical remedies of the time. The result? Paralysis in their hind legs.

Jonas Salk never said his polio vaccine caused polio. He developed an inactivated (killed-virus) polio vaccine and believed it was the safest approach, as a killed virus cannot cause the disease. A major vaccine safety incident, known as the "Cutter incident," happened with his vaccine, but it was due to manufacturing errors, not a flaw in his core design. Salk's killed-virus approach: Salk's vaccine was intentionally created with a killed poliovirus to eliminate the risk of the virus causing paralysis. This contrasted with Albert Sabin's later oral vaccine, which used a live, weakened virus that could, in very rare instances, revert and cause paralysis.
The Cutter incident: In 1955, shortly after the Salk vaccine was approved, a pharmaceutical company named Cutter Laboratories improperly manufactured some batches of the vaccine.
Live virus contamination: The manufacturing process failed to completely kill the poliovirus, which resulted in live, active virus being present in some vaccine doses.
Resulting cases: This led to thousands of children being exposed to the live virus. Over 200 children experienced varying degrees of paralysis, and 10 died. Salk not at fault: Investigations determined that the problem was not Salk's design but a manufacturing defect that did not follow his specific inactivation instructions. Production was quickly suspended, and corrective safety measures were put in place.

For over ten years, Thorsen was “number one” on the U.S. Health and Human Services (HHS) “most-wanted list.”

He was indicted by a federal grand jury in 2011 on 22 counts of wire fraud and money laundering.

However, he managed to avoid extradition to America after Denmark refused to hand him over.

Now, thanks to an Interpol Red Notice, the fugitive has finally been caught.

The Man Behind Flawed Vaccine Studies

Thorsen’s papers were instrumental in shaping U.S. vaccine policy.

His findings, claiming no link between vaccines and autism, were cited by the Institute of Medicine.

They were notably used to deny settlements to more than 5,000 families in the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program (VICP).

Critics have long argued the studies were deeply flawed, with some now suggesting outright fabrication.

Dr. James Lyons-Weiler, Ph.D., said in a detailed analysis:

“Results fabrication in the Danish registry results seems all but certain, given the clear evidence of those practices in other studies on the topic of vaccines and autism.”

Thorsen’s research was funded through CDC grants worth over $11 million between 2000 and 2009.

Instead of ensuring rigorous research, prosecutors allege he submitted fraudulent invoices, siphoning hundreds of thousands of dollars into personal accounts.

The stolen money was allegedly spent on purchasing a home in Atlanta, a Harley Davidson motorcycle, cars, and luxury items.

Kennedy: Thorsen Was HHS’s ‘Number One Fugitive’

HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who has repeatedly blasted Thorsen’s role in cementing vaccine orthodoxy, confirmed that the fugitive scientist had topped the department’s most-wanted list for years.

“Thorsen has been number one on the Health and Human Services (HHS) most wanted list for the past 10 years,” Kennedy told Breitbart.

The Fallout

The arrest raises serious questions about the entire body of vaccine-autism research tied to Thorsen and his colleagues.

His co-authored Danish registry studies are now under renewed scrutiny for:

Misclassified and shifting diagnostic categories
Reporting biases and conflicts of interest
Distorted data, vulnerable to confounding variables
Critics argue the CDC-backed studies formed the bedrock of the “safe and effective” narrative while potentially hiding evidence of harm.
Lyons-Weiler called for urgent action:

Full transparency of Thorsen’s datasets
Independent replication of all findings
Reexamination of policies built on his work
What Happens Next

Thorsen faces 13 counts of wire fraud and 9 counts of money laundering, each carrying lengthy prison sentences and heavy fines.

U.S. prosecutors are also seeking forfeiture of all property purchased with the stolen money.

If extradited, the trial could expose not only financial fraud, but the possibility of scientific fraud at the heart of vaccine safety claims.

For years, mainstream media dismissed concerns about Thorsen’s work as “conspiracy theories.”

His arrest may now prove critics right and reveal that the “settled science” used to silence vaccine injury claims may itself have been tainted by fraud.

The history of Polio.
Without question the go-to disease to defend vaccines is polio. Coincidently it's also the greatest lie and medical con job of all time. Toxins Causing "Polio" is well documented. Polio is the virus that never was.

1824: Metal workers had suffered for centuries from a paralysis similar to polio caused by the lead and arsenic in the metals they were working with. English scientist John Cooke observed: 'The fumes from these metals, or the receptance of them in solution into the stomach, often causes paralysis.'

1890: Lead arsenate pesticide started to be sprayed in the US up to 12 times every summer to kill codling moth on apple crops.

1892: Polio outbreaks began to occur in Vermont, an apple growing region. In his report the Government Inspector Dr. Charles Caverly noted that parents reported that some children fell ill after eating fruit. He stated that 'infantile paralysis usually occurred in families with more than one child, and as no efforts were made at isolation it was very certain it was non-contagious' (with only one child in the family having been struck).

1907: Calcium arsenate comes into use primarily on cotton crops.

1908: In a Massachusetts town with three cotton mills and apple orchards, 69 children suddenly fell ill with infantile paralysis.

1909: The UK bans apple imports from the States because of heavy lead arsenate residues.

1921: Franklin D. Roosevelt develops polio after swimming in Bay of Fundy, New Brunswick. Toxicity of water may have been due to pollution run-off.

1943: DDT is introduced, a neurotoxic pesticide. Over the next several years it comes into widespread use in American households. For example, wall paper impregnated with DDT was placed in children's bedrooms.

1943: A polio epidemic in the UK town of Broadstairs, Kent is linked to a local dairy where cows were washed down with DDT.

1944: Albert Sabin reports that a major cause of sickness and death of American troops based in the Philippines was poliomyelitis. US military camps there were sprayed daily with DDT to kill mosquitoes. Neighboring Philippine settlements were not affected.

1944: NIH reports that DDT damages the same anterior horn cells that are damaged in infantile paralysis.

1946: Gebhaedt shows polio seasonality correlates with fruit harvest.

1949: Endocrinologist Dr Morton Biskind, a practitioner and medical researcher, found that DDT causes 'lesions in the spinal cord similar to human polio.'

1950: US Public Health Industrial Hygiene Medical Director, J.G. Townsend, notes the similarity between parathion poisoning and polio and believes that some polio might be caused by eating fruits or vegetables with parathion residues.

1951: Dr. Biskind treats his polio patients as poisoning victims, removing toxins from food and environment, especially DDT contaminated milk and butter. Dr. Biskind writes: 'Although young animals are more susceptible to the effects of DDT than adults, so far as the available literature is concerned, it does not appear that the effects of such concentrations on infants and children have even been considered.'

1949-1951: Other doctors report they are having success treating polio with anti toxins used to treat poisoning, dimercaprol and ascorbic acid. Example: Dr. F. R. Klenner reported: 'In the poliomyelitis epidemic in North Carolina in 1948 60 cases of this disease came under our care... The treatment was massive doses of vitamin C every two to four hours. Children up to four years received vitamin C injection intramuscularly... All patients were clinically well after 72 hours.'

1950: Dr. Biskind presents evidence to the US Congress that pesticides were the major cause of polio epidemics. He is joined by Dr. Ralph Scobey who reported he found clear evidence of poisoning when analyzing chemical traces in the blood of polio victims.

Comment: This was a no no. The viral causation theory was not something to be questioned. The careers of prominent virologists and health authorities were threatened. Biskind and Scobey's ideas were subjected to ridicule.

1953: Clothes are moth-proofed by washing them in EQ-53, a formula containing DDT.

1953: Dr. Biskind writes: 'It was known by 1945 that DDT was stored in the body fat of mammals and appears in their milk... yet far from admitting a causal relationship between DDT and polio that is so obvious, which in any other field of biology would be instantly accepted, virtually the entire apparatus of communication, lay and scientific alike, has been devoted to denying, concealing, suppressing, distorting and attempts to convert into its opposite this overwhelming evidence. Pesticides were used decades before polio developed. To blame polio on pesticides is disingenuous at best.
Many organochloride compounds, such as BHC and DDT, were first synthesized in the 1800s, but their properties as insecticides were not fully discovered and exploited until the late 1930s. BHC (Benzene hexachloride) was first produced by the English scientist Michael Faraday in 1825, but its properties as an insecticide were not identified until 1944. DDT (dichlorodipheny|trichloroethane) was first prepared by Othmar Ziedler, an Austrian chemist, in 1825, but the Swiss chemist Paul Hermann Müller did not discover DDT's insecticidal properties until 1939 - a discovery that led to Müller's award of the Nobel Prize in 1948.

This post is misleading. The 1954 Salk polio vaccine trial (the “Francis Field Trial”) was one of the largest medical studies ever, involving over 1.8 million children. Yes, it showed no significant reduction in non-paralytic polio (the mild, flu-like form), but the real result was clear: the vaccine reduced paralytic polio by roughly 80–90%. That’s why cases of children ending up in iron lungs plummeted after rollout — from over 15,000 paralytic cases a year in the 1950s to near zero within a generation.

Cherry-picking “non-paralytic” outcomes while ignoring the dramatic protection against paralysis is like claiming seatbelts don’t work because they don’t stop fender benders, while leaving out that they save lives in crashes.

If this group is really full of physicians, the fact they don’t grasp such a basic point about trial endpoints and medical informatics shows they’re either willfully misleading people or they’ve forgotten the first lecture of Epidemiology 101.

LAGOS COMMENCES MASS DEWORMING OF SCHOOL-AGE CHILDREN

... Targets 1.3 Million in 10 Endemic LGAs

Lagos State Government, through the Ministry of Health and in partnership with a non-governmental Organisation, Evidence Action, on Tuesday, commenced mass deworming for the treatment and prevention of Soil-Transmitted Helminths (STH) in 10 Local Government Areas of the State.

Speaking during the official flag-off of the exercise held at St. Peters Anglican Primary School, Alausa, the Commissioner for Health, Prof. Akin Abayomi explained that Soil-Transmitted Helminths (STH), which is prevalent in school-age children. is a significant infectious public health burden in Nigeria that results from poor sanitation and hygiene conditions.

According to him, STH is a worm infection that interferes with nutrient uptake and can lead to anaemia, malnourishment and impaired mental, physical and cognitive development, which usually pose a serious threat to children’s health, education and productivity.

He said, “Previously, there have been small-scale deworming activities in the State against STH and Schistosomiasis. However, with the support of the Federal Ministry of Health and our supporting partner, Evidence Action, we have set out plans to implement a Statewide deworming treatment for all enrolled and unenrolled school-age children, who are at risk, using a coordinated approach called School-Based Deworming Mass Administration of Medicines”.

The Commissioner, who was represented by the Lagos State Coordinator, Neglected Tropical Diseases, NTD, Mr. Olanrewaju Jenrola, noted that the treatment strategy is based on the prevalence of STH being determined by mapping and scientific survey as spelt out by the guidelines from Nigeria’s Federal Ministry of Health and the World Health Organisation.

Mary S. Sherman, a prominent orthopedic surgeon and cancer researcher, was involved in medical work related to polio and its potential long-term health consequences. She served as an associate professor of orthopedic surgery at the University of Chicago, where her research focused on bone cancer and polio treatments.
 In the late 1940s and early 1950s, she traveled to Alaska to conduct medical surveys and provide care in remote communities, including at Mount Edgecumbe Hospital on Kruzof Island.
Her later career included a significant role at the Ochsner Clinic in New Orleans, where she worked on cancer research and was associated with Dr. Alton Ochsner.
 According to conspiracy theories popularized by Edward T. Haslam in his book Dr. Mary's Monkey, Sherman was involved in secret research related to the polio vaccine's contamination with the SV-40 virus, a monkey virus known to cause cancer in humans.
 Haslam theorizes that the contaminated polio vaccine, which was grown on monkey kidney cells, led to an epidemic of soft-tissue cancers, prompting a covert government-backed research program to develop a vaccine to counteract this threat.
 This research allegedly involved the use of a linear particle accelerator at the Public Health Service Hospital in New Orleans.
The theory suggests that Sherman's work on this project, which included experimenting with cancer-causing monkey viruses, led to her murder in July 1964.
 Her body was found severely burned, with her right arm and part of her torso disintegrated, an injury that Haslam claims could not have resulted from a small fire in her apartment.
 He argues that the burn patterns were consistent with an accident involving the linear particle accelerator, and that her death was covered up to conceal the secret research.
 While forensic pathologists dispute this account, stating that a normal fire could have caused the observed burns, the theory remains a central element of the unresolved mystery surrounding her death.
The connection between Sherman and the polio vaccine controversy is further highlighted by the fact that the SV-40 virus, which was present in some early polio vaccines, has been linked to various cancers, including soft-tissue cancers, and has been a subject of ongoing medical debate.
 The controversy surrounding the vaccine's contamination and its potential long-term health effects remains a significant point of discussion in medical history.

We don’t live in 1905 bathing in sewage water…”

✅ True: modern sanitation and clean water dramatically reduced many infectious diseases — including polio  before and alongside vaccines.

❌ But incomplete: clean water alone didn’t eliminate polio. Polio spread primarily via the fecal-oral route, and as sanitation improved, paradoxically, average age of infection increased  meaning children were no longer exposed early when maternal antibodies could protect them. This is part of why major outbreaks occurred in wealthier nations in the 1940s–1950s.

📊 “75%+ of ‘polio infections’ have zero symptoms; paralysis <1%.”

✅ Correct: most polio infections are asymptomatic. Only about 0.1–1% result in paralysis.

❌ But significance matters: polio’s paralysis rate may seem small, but in a large population, that still translated to tens of thousands of paralyzed children each year in the pre-vaccine era. In 1952 alone, the U.S. saw >21,000 cases of paralytic polio and 3,000 deaths, even with modern sanitation by that time.

🧬 “Most modern paralytic cases are vaccine-derived.”

✅ True in part: since wild poliovirus has been eliminated in most regions, vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV) is now the dominant cause of new cases.

❌ Why: this happens in areas with low vaccination coverage, allowing the weakened live virus from the oral vaccine to circulate and mutate. Countries using the inactivated polio vaccine (IPV)  like the U.S. since 2000 cannot cause VDPV because it uses a killed virus.

☠️ “DDT and pesticides caused the ‘polio spikes.’”.

❌ There’s no credible scientific evidence that DDT or arsenic caused polio.

Polio virus has been isolated from patients and animals long before widespread pesticide use.

Polio epidemics correlated with viral transmission patterns, not pesticide spraying records.

Controlled studies and pathology show viral infection of motor neurons, not chemical toxicity.

💉 “Modern U.S. polio risk = basically zero.”

✅ Yes: thanks to high vaccine coverage, sanitation, and surveillance, the U.S. has been polio-free since 1979.

❌ But that remains true only because of ongoing vaccination. If vaccination rates drop, vaccine-derived strains can reemerge, as seen in New York in 2022, where an unvaccinated adult developed paralytic polio from circulating VDPV.

⚖️ “My job is to make a risk–benefit calculation for my kids.”

✅ Absolutely right: informed consent means understanding both benefits and risks.
But the data show that the benefit of vaccination (preventing lifelong paralysis) far outweighs the tiny risk of vaccine complications, which are millions of times rarer than polio’s natural complications.

When I was six, my dad played a game with me. He’d state a “fact,” and my job was to disprove it.
“All cars have four wheels,” he once said.
“No, Dad!” I’d fire back. “Cars on the assembly line don’t. Cars that just had an accident don’t. Cars being shipped on trucks might not. Some quirky European cars have three wheels. And what do you even mean by ‘car’? Does that include SUVs? What about vehicles with multiple axles or dual tires?”
I loved that game.
Here’s the problem—and it dismantles every blanket pro-vaccine argument: you can never say, with intellectual honesty, “Vaccines work.” That phrase assumes two things:
That all vaccines are the same.
That “work” has one universally accepted definition.
Neither is true.
Vaccines differ vastly in design, target, effectiveness, and safety profile. Each must be assessed scientifically, individually, on its own merits, flaws, and evidence. 

Saying “vaccines work” is as sloppy as saying “all cars have four wheels.”

Lat’s stop using blanket, absolute statements in regard to highly nuanced issues. 

“Notorious Vaccine Failures – Historical Examples
There is no single vaccine universally recognized as the 'worst of all time,' because different vaccines have failed for different reasons—sometimes due to scientific limitations of the era, sometimes due to poor trial design, manufacturing errors, or political misuse. However, a few vaccines stand out historically for their notable failures in effectiveness, safety, or both.

1. 1960s RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus) Vaccine
Why it failed: In 1966, a formalin-inactivated RSV vaccine was given to children. Not only did it fail to protect them, it caused enhanced disease when they later encountered RSV naturally.

Impact: 80% of vaccinated children in trials were hospitalized when they caught RSV, and two died.

Lesson: A vaccine can make a disease worse if the immune response it triggers is not the right kind.

2. 1976 “Swine Flu” Vaccine (U.S.)
Why it failed: The U.S. government rushed to vaccinate ~45 million people against a swine flu strain feared to cause a pandemic. That pandemic never materialized.

Impact: The vaccine was linked to an increased risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome (a rare paralytic condition), leading to public mistrust.

Lesson: Premature mass vaccination without strong evidence of necessity can cause more harm than benefit.

3. Dengvaxia (Dengue Fever Vaccine) – Philippines
Why it failed: Approved in 2015, it was given to ~800,000 schoolchildren. Later data showed it increased the risk of severe dengue in those who had never been previously infected.

Impact: Dozens of deaths were politically attributed to the vaccine, leading to criminal charges and a collapse of public trust in vaccination programs nationwide.

Lesson: Pre-screening for prior infection is crucial when vaccine risks depend on immune history.

4. Cutter Incident – 1955 Polio Vaccine (Salk)
Why it failed: A manufacturing error at Cutter Laboratories led to polio vaccines that contained live poliovirus.

Impact: 40,000 cases of polio, 200 cases of paralysis, and 10 deaths.

Lesson: Even an effective vaccine can be a disaster if quality control fails.

Examples of Low-Effectiveness Vaccines
• Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) Tuberculosis Vaccine — offers inconsistent protection in adults (0–80% efficacy depending on region) but still widely given in many countries.

• Current influenza vaccines — effectiveness often hovers between 10–60% depending on the year’s strain match.”

That’s a great and important question — especially for travelers who go abroad often.

Here’s how it works:

✅ If you’ve already completed your primary polio vaccine series (the 3 or 4 childhood doses), you generally only need one lifetime booster dose of the inactivated polio vaccine (IPV) as an adult.

The World Health Organization (WHO) and the U.S. CDC both recommend just one booster in adulthood for travelers to areas where polio still occurs or is a risk.

You don’t need to get a polio shot every year unless a specific country’s entry requirement or your International Certificate of Vaccination (Yellow Card) requires an updated dose for documentation purposes.

✅ For frequent travelers:
If you travel to countries that still have polio outbreaks (for example, parts of Africa or Asia), authorities may ask for a recent vaccination record (within the past 12 months), especially for long stays. In that case, it’s about entry regulation, not your immunity level — your previous booster still protects you, but you might need a new certificate.

Summary:

Immunity-wise: One adult polio booster is enough for life.

Travel-wise: Some countries may require proof of vaccination within 1 year for entry or exit — so check each destination’s rules.
